content.text: A discriminative model learns to predict an output y directly from an input x by modelling the conditional distribution p(y | x). Rather than asking "how was this data generated?", it asks "given this input, what is the most likely label?" This narrow focus on the decision boundary — rather than the full data distribution — makes discriminative models highly effective for classification and regression tasks, and they are the dominant paradigm in supervised learning.
A discriminative model parameterises p(y | x; θ) directly. For binary classification with labels y ∈ {0, 1}, logistic regression is the canonical example:
For multi-class classification with y ∈ {1, …, K}, the standard choice is a softmax over K linear projections:
The key point is that neither formula requires knowing p(x) — the marginal distribution of inputs. The model focuses entirely on the conditional relationship between x and y.
Given a labelled dataset {(x_i, y_i)}_{i=1}^n, training maximises the conditional log-likelihood:

This is equivalent to minimising the cross-entropy loss, which measures how well the predicted distribution over labels matches the true label for each example. The model is never asked to assign probability to x itself — only to y given x. This allows discriminative models to ignore irrelevant variation in the input space and focus entirely on features that separate classes.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch import Tensor


class DiscriminativeClassifier(nn.Module):
    """
    A discriminative classifier that directly models p(y | x).
    The forward pass outputs a conditional distribution over K classes
    given input x — it makes no attempt to model p(x).
    """
    def __init__(self, input_dim: int, n_classes: int, hidden_dim: int = 256):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, n_classes),   # logits for each class
        )

    def forward(self, x: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        return self.net(x)   # (B, K) unnormalised logits

    def predict_proba(self, x: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        """Returns p(y | x) as a proper probability distribution."""
        return torch.softmax(self.forward(x), dim=-1)   # (B, K)

    def predict(self, x: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        """Returns argmax_y p(y | x): the most likely class."""
        return self.forward(x).argmax(dim=-1)   # (B,)


def train_step(
    model: DiscriminativeClassifier,
    optimizer: torch.optim.Optimizer,
    x: Tensor,
    y: Tensor,  # ground-truth class indices (B,)
) -> float:
    """One gradient step on the conditional log-likelihood (cross-entropy)."""
    logits = model(x)                           # (B, K)
    loss = nn.functional.cross_entropy(logits, y)  # -log p(y | x) averaged
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
    return loss.item()

The defining property of a discriminative model is that it learns a decision boundary in input space — the surface where p(y = 1 | x) = 0.5 — rather than the full data distribution. In a 2-class problem, logistic regression finds a linear hyperplane; deep neural networks find complex nonlinear boundaries. The model can be highly accurate in the regions where training data is dense and completely undefined in regions with no training examples — this is acceptable because the model is never asked to generate or evaluate the plausibility of new x values.

This also explains why discriminative models are vulnerable to out-of-distribution inputs: a classifier trained to separate cats from dogs may confidently misclassify a noise image as one or the other, because nothing in its training objective prevents it from assigning high probability to nonsensical inputs.
Why Discriminative Models Usually Win at Classification

Given the same number of parameters, discriminative models tend to outperform generative models on classification benchmarks. The theoretical explanation (Vapnik's principle): if you want to predict y from x, solve that problem directly — do not solve the harder problem of modelling p(x, y) as an intermediate step. Modelling p(x) requires handling all the variation in the input space (pose, lighting, texture, etc. for images), which consumes capacity that would be better spent on the classification boundary.

Empirically, this plays out in two ways. First, discriminative models reach higher accuracy with less data in the supervised regime. Second, they converge faster in training because the loss signal is always label-aligned — every gradient step directly improves the mapping from x to y, rather than also trying to reconstruct or model x.
The Asymmetry of the Conditional Distribution
A discriminative model p(y | x) is inherently asymmetric: it produces a distribution over y given x, but not over x given y. This has practical consequences:
Cannot generate new inputs x ~ p(x | y). A cat-dog classifier cannot produce a new image of a cat.
Cannot handle missing features without imputation — p(y | x) requires all features of x to be present (or must be redesigned to marginalise over missing values).
Cannot detect out-of-distribution inputs — the model assigns p(y | x) for any x, including nonsensical ones, with no built-in mechanism to flag that x is implausible.
Cannot be used for density estimation or anomaly detection without augmenting with a separate p(x) model.

Discriminative models extend naturally to structured output spaces where y is a sequence, tree, or graph rather than a single class label. Sequence-to-sequence models, conditional random fields, and object detectors are all discriminative: they model p(y | x) where y is an entire sequence of tokens, a sequence of named-entity tags, or a set of bounding boxes.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch import Tensor


class Seq2SeqDiscriminative(nn.Module):
    """
    Sequence-to-sequence discriminative model: directly models p(y | x)
    for structured output y = (y_1, ..., y_T) given input sequence x.
    An encoder-decoder Transformer is this type — it predicts the next token
    conditioned on the source x and all previous target tokens (teacher forcing).
    """
    def __init__(self, vocab_size: int, d_model: int = 256, n_heads: int = 4):
        super().__init__()
        self.encoder = nn.TransformerEncoder(
            nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(d_model, n_heads, batch_first=True),
            num_layers=3,
        )
        self.decoder = nn.TransformerDecoder(
            nn.TransformerDecoderLayer(d_model, n_heads, batch_first=True),
            num_layers=3,
        )
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, d_model)
        self.out = nn.Linear(d_model, vocab_size)

    def forward(self, src: Tensor, tgt: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        """
        src: (B, S) source token ids
        tgt: (B, T) target token ids (teacher-forced)
        returns: (B, T, vocab_size) logits for p(y_t | x, y_<t)
        """
        memory = self.encoder(self.embed(src))    # (B, S, d_model)
        out = self.decoder(self.embed(tgt), memory)  # (B, T, d_model)
        return self.out(out)                       # (B, T, vocab_size)

    def conditional_nll(self, src: Tensor, tgt: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        """
        Negative log-likelihood: -log p(y | x) = sum over t of -log p(y_t | x, y_<t)
        This is the discriminative training objective.
        """
        logits = self.forward(src, tgt[:, :-1])   # (B, T-1, V)
        targets = tgt[:, 1:]                       # (B, T-1) shifted right
        return nn.functional.cross_entropy(
            logits.reshape(-1, logits.size(-1)),
            targets.reshape(-1),
        )
Discriminative vs Generative: When Each Wins
The Boundary Blurs: Discriminative Pre-training + Fine-tuning

Modern practice often combines both paradigms. BERT is pre-trained generatively (masked language modelling: predict masked tokens, which is a discriminative objective over the vocabulary conditioned on context) and then fine-tuned discriminatively (a classification head on top). GPT models use a purely generative pre-training (autoregressive language modelling) and then a discriminative fine-tuning (RLHF, instruction tuning) to align them to user intent. The distinction is most useful as a conceptual framework for understanding what a model can and cannot do, rather than as a rigid architectural classification.

Mental model: a discriminative model is a judge that has studied thousands of court cases and learned to rule confidently on any case presented to it — but cannot write a story about what happened. A generative model is a novelist who understands how events unfold and can imagine new scenarios — but is not necessarily better at classifying existing ones. The judge (discriminative) is better at its narrow task precisely because it has never had to learn to write.
A discriminative model directly parameterises p(y | x; θ), optimised by maximising conditional log-likelihood (equivalent to minimising cross-entropy).
It learns a decision boundary — not a data distribution — which makes it more data-efficient for supervised classification than generative alternatives.
The trade-off is inability to generate new inputs x, handle missing features naturally, or detect out-of-distribution data without supplementary mechanisms.
The class spans logistic regression, SVMs, CNN/MLP classifiers, fine-tuned BERT, CRFs, and seq2seq models — any model whose training objective is directly p(y | x).
In modern LLM practice, the discriminative/generative boundary blurs: generative pre-training followed by discriminative fine-tuning (RLHF, instruction tuning) is the dominant pipeline.
